title: Image Generation Platform — System Design
description: Design a text-to-image generation service: take a prompt, run an expensive GPU diffusion model, and return a safe image. The themes are async GPU job processing on scarce hardware, priority/fair scheduling and batching, input/output safety, and result delivery.

nodes.label: Client
nodes.description: Submits a text prompt and polls/streams for the generated image.

nodes.label: Generation API
nodes.description: Accepts prompts, enforces quota, runs input safety, enqueues jobs.

nodes.label: Job Store
nodes.description: Authoritative job records, status, and result references.

nodes.label: Input Safety Filter
nodes.description: Blocks disallowed prompts before any GPU work.

nodes.label: Priority Job Queues
nodes.description: Per-tier (free/paid/realtime) queues feeding the GPU workers.

nodes.label: GPU Scheduler
nodes.description: Assigns jobs to GPU workers; batches and honors priority.

nodes.label: GPU Inference Workers
nodes.description: Run the diffusion model on a GPU to produce images.

nodes.label: Model / Weights Store
nodes.description: Versioned model weights loaded by workers.

nodes.label: Output Safety Filter
nodes.description: Classifies generated images; blocks/flags unsafe output.

nodes.label: Notification / Stream
nodes.description: Pushes completion (or streams progress) to the client.

functional: Submit a text prompt (with options: size, count, model) and get generated image(s).
Screen prompts and outputs for disallowed content.
Show job status; deliver results when ready (poll or push).
Support tiers/priorities (free vs paid vs realtime).
nonFunctional: GPUs are scarce and expensive — maximize utilization, never idle them.
Generation takes seconds, so processing is async; the request returns a job id fast.
Safety is mandatory on input and output.
Fairness: a flood of free-tier requests must not starve paid/realtime users.
Resilient: a worker or job failure must not lose the request or strand a GPU.

capacity.label: Example workload
capacity.note: ~1M requests/day, avg n≈2 images/request. Mix: ~70% free, ~25% paid, ~5% realtime; ~80% at 1024px, ~20% at 512px.

capacity.label: Peak submit QPS
capacity.note: Bursty: ~24 images/sec average, but diurnal + viral peaks push 5–10× the mean for short windows. At ~200 submits/sec with n≈2 that's ~400 images/sec incoming.

capacity.label: Admission / backlog policy
capacity.value: provision for SLO, queue the rest
capacity.note: Serving 400 images/sec immediately would need ~357 GPUs (400 ÷ 1.6 ÷ 0.7) — far above the ~21-GPU steady fleet. We don't size for peak: reserve a realtime/paid slice (steady fleet + a burst pool to ~100 GPUs) and let free-tier queue or shed under burst. Realtime/paid are admission-limited to their reserved capacity; free is best-effort with ETA or throttling.

capacity.label: Seconds per image
capacity.note: Diffusion is step-bound; a batch of 16 runs in ~10 s, so batching gives ~6× the per-GPU throughput of single-image passes.

capacity.label: Images/sec/GPU
capacity.note: 16-image batch in ~10 s ≈ 1.6 images/sec/GPU. At ~24 images/sec mean and 70% target utilization that's ~21 GPUs steady-state, headroom to ~100+ for peaks.

capacity.label: Queue wait by tier
capacity.value: realtime <2 s p95 · paid <30 s p95 · free best-effort
capacity.note: These SLOs are enforced by admission control, not by over-provisioning: realtime/paid are capped to their reserved GPU slice so their waits stay bounded; free drains from the remaining capacity and absorbs backlog (minutes under burst), with an ETA shown rather than dropped work.

capacity.label: Result size & storage
capacity.note: 2M images/day × ~1.5 MB. 30-day retention ≈ ~90 TB object-store footprint before tier-based expiry.

capacity.label: CDN egress
capacity.note: Each image fetched 2–5× (UI render, downloads, shares); CDN absorbs the read fanout so workers/origin never serve it.

capacity.label: GPUs
capacity.note: The bottleneck resource; utilization is the headline metric.

api.description: Submit a generation job. Returns a job id immediately; processing is async. An Idempotency-Key header dedupes client retries (same key + fingerprint returns the original job, never a new one). Optional callbackUrl registers a per-job completion webhook: on completion the service POSTs a signed event { eventId, jobId, status, images? } to that URL, HMAC-signed with the caller's secret and retried with backoff (dead-lettered after N attempts); consumers dedupe on eventId. For callers that prefer not to register a URL per request, an account-level endpoint can be configured out of band — the per-request callbackUrl is the in-scope path here.

Poll job status / get result URLs when ready. Authorized to the owning user (or API client); returns status, reason, timestamps, model version, and per-image delivery metadata. Image URLs are short-lived signed CDN links scoped to the owner.

Cancel a queued or running job (owner-authorized). If still queued, it's removed from the queue and marked canceled — no GPU is spent. If inference has already started, cancellation is best-effort: the current attempt's lease is allowed to finish (the GPU pass is non-preemptible mid-batch) but the result is discarded and not delivered. Idempotent: canceling an already-terminal job returns its current state.

User-requested deletion of a generation and its images (owner-authorized). Tombstones the generated_images rows (sets deleted_at), purges the objects from the result store, and invalidates CDN cache / outstanding signed URLs. The job record is retained (in a deleted state) for audit/billing until its retention window expires.

dataModel.name: jobs
dataModel.note: Authoritative job record and lifecycle; JobDB is the source of truth, not the queue or scheduler.

fields.name: model_version
fields.type: text (pinned at submit or first run)

fields.name: status
fields.type: enum(queued, running, done, blocked_input, blocked_output, failed, canceled, deleted)

dataModel.name: job_attempts
dataModel.note: One row per execution try; supports lease/fencing, crash recovery, and dead-lettering.

dataModel.name: idempotency_keys
dataModel.note: Submit-dedup so a retrying client never spawns a duplicate GPU job.

dataModel.name: outbox_events
dataModel.note: Transactional outbox bridging the JobDB→queue gap: written in the same transaction as the job, so an accepted job always has a durable, recoverable path into scheduling even if the API crashes before publishing.

fields.name: payload
fields.type: json (job_id + tier only; worker fetches the rest from JobDB)

dataModel.name: generated_images
dataModel.note: Per-image artifact metadata; replaces the opaque result_keys list.

dataModel.name: safety_decisions
dataModel.note: Audit trail for input and output moderation; explainable for support, abuse review, and compliance.

dataModel.name: model_versions
dataModel.note: Model catalog and rollout state; the model_version pinned on a job (at submit) resolves here so batching, safety policy, and reproducibility are version-aware.

dataModel.name: webhook_deliveries
dataModel.note: Reliable server-to-server completion callbacks with signing, retry, and dead-letter.

dataModel.name: quotas
dataModel.note: Per-user limits and fair-share accounting; richer than a single remaining integer.

patterns.name: Async job + status
patterns.what: Accept the request, return a job id, process on the GPU fleet asynchronously.
patterns.whenToUse: Expensive work (seconds+) that can't block the request.

patterns.name: Pre-inference safety gate
patterns.what: Screen the prompt before spending any GPU; screen the output before delivery.
patterns.group: Safety Gates on Input and Output

patterns.name: Priority / fair scheduling
patterns.what: Per-tier queues + fair-share so paid/realtime aren't starved by free traffic.
patterns.whenToUse: Multi-tier demand on a scarce shared resource.

patterns.name: Inference batching
patterns.what: Group compatible jobs into one GPU batch to amortize model/overhead cost.
patterns.whenToUse: GPU inference where batching raises throughput dramatically.

patterns.name: Result store + CDN delivery
patterns.what: Store generated images in object storage and serve via CDN; notify on completion.
patterns.whenToUse: Large binary results read after async completion.

patterns.name: Idempotent retryable jobs
patterns.what: Jobs are keyed and re-runnable so a worker crash doesn't lose or duplicate work.
patterns.whenToUse: Long GPU jobs on a fleet that can fail mid-run.

steps.title: 1. Naive: Run the Model Synchronously in the Request
description: Start with the simplest thing: when a prompt arrives, the API calls the diffusion model right there inside the request handler, waits for the GPU to produce the image (seconds), and returns it in the HTTP response. No queue, no job id — request in, image out.

This is acceptable only for a toy demo or internal prototype — a single user, no bursts, no safety requirements. For bursty user-facing traffic it collapses immediately because GPUs are slow and scarce. Each request holds an HTTP connection (and a GPU) for many seconds, so a burst instantly exhausts the GPU pool while everyone else's connections pile up and time out. There's no place to absorb the burst, no way to prioritize a paying user over a flood of free traffic, and no safety gate before expensive compute.
So the synchronous call motivates the whole async pipeline: accept and enqueue a job fast, schedule scarce GPUs fairly, batch inference, and screen for safety before and after generation.

view.caption: The client submits a prompt to the generation API, which runs the diffusion model inline on the GPU workers and blocks until the image returns.

steps.decisionPrompt: Each image takes ~10 seconds on a GPU and you have 8 GPUs. What happens when 1,000 prompts arrive in a few seconds and the API generates them inline?
whyNow: Running the model inline makes the GPU bottleneck visceral — connections pile up and time out — which is exactly why the design accepts a job fast, queues it, and schedules scarce GPUs.

recap.after: The API runs the diffusion model inside the request and returns the image directly.
recap.newRisk: Slow, scarce GPUs mean requests hold connections for seconds, bursts exhaust the pool and time out, and there's no prioritization or safety gating.

traps.trap: Generating the image synchronously inside the request.
traps.why: Multi-second GPU work holds connections open, a burst exhausts the GPU pool and times out, and there's nowhere to prioritize or screen.
traps.instead: Accept + persist + enqueue a job, return a job id fast, and process on the GPU fleet asynchronously.

steps.title: 2. Async Intake: Accept, Persist, Enqueue
description: Generation takes seconds, so it can't be synchronous. The API validates the request, checks the user's quota, persists a job record, and enqueues it — returning a job id immediately (202).
Persisting before enqueueing means a crash never loses an accepted job; the queue is rebuildable from queued jobs. The client polls or subscribes for completion.
Make submit idempotent: a client Idempotency-Key (or a user_id + prompt/params fingerprint) is stored with the job so a retry returns the original job id instead of spawning a duplicate GPU run.

The durability invariant: every accepted job (every 202) has exactly one durable JobDB record and at least one recoverable path into scheduling. JobDB is the source of truth; the queue is operational and rebuildable. The failure-prone part is the write→publish bridge — if the API writes the job but crashes before publishing to the queue, the job must not be stranded. Close that gap with a transactional outbox: write the job and an outbox_events row in one DB transaction, then have a relay publish pending outbox rows to the queue and mark them published. Back it with a periodic sweeper that re-enqueues jobs stuck in queued with no live attempt. The queue message carries only job_id (+ tier); the worker fetches the authoritative prompt/params from JobDB, so a duplicate or stale queue message can't desync the source of truth.
This async-job shape is the backbone: it decouples request rate from the (much smaller) GPU throughput.

view.caption: The generation API persists each job to the job store and enqueues it on the per-tier priority queues, returning a job id immediately.

steps.decisionPrompt: Why return a job id instead of holding the request open until the image is ready?

concepts.definition: Accept + persist + enqueue, return an id, process later, deliver async.
concepts.whyItMatters: Keeps the request fast and absorbs bursts the GPU fleet can't serve instantly.
concepts.example: POST -> 202 {jobId}; client polls GET until status=done.

whyNow: The async-job decision shapes the whole system; it's what lets scarce GPUs be a queue-fed resource rather than a request-blocking one.

recap.after: Jobs durably accepted and queued; fast response.
recap.newRisk: Running disallowed prompts wastes scarce GPU and risks policy violations — screen first.

traps.trap: Enqueueing on every submit with no dedup, and publishing to the queue outside the DB transaction.
traps.why: A retrying client spawns duplicate GPU jobs (expensive), and a crash between the DB write and the queue publish leaves an accepted job that never runs.
traps.instead: Dedupe on an idempotency key / request fingerprint, and bridge the write→publish gap with a transactional outbox or a re-enqueue sweeper.

flows.name: Async intake: screen, persist, enqueue
flows.note: The API screens the prompt before any GPU work; an allowed prompt is persisted and enqueued by tier and a job id returns immediately, while a disallowed prompt is rejected at intake.

steps.title: 3. Safety Gates on Input and Output
description: Safety is mandatory and bookends the pipeline. Screen the prompt before any GPU work: a disallowed prompt is rejected at intake, saving expensive compute and preventing policy violations.
Screen the output too: classify the generated image and block/flag unsafe results before they're delivered, since a benign-looking prompt can still produce disallowed content.

Input screening is cheap and synchronous at intake; output screening runs right after inference, before the result is stored/served. Blocked is a terminal state with reason codes: distinguish blocked_input from blocked_output, and store the policy version, classifier version, decision, score, and reason for each check in a safety_decisions audit log so support, abuse review, and compliance can explain any block.

Operational policy: handle adversarial/jailbreak prompts and throttle repeat violators using abuse telemetry. Decide what happens to an unsafe generated image — delete immediately, or route to a restricted review bucket keyed by classifier metadata. When the safety service is down, too slow, or returns uncertain, fail closed (block or route to review) rather than fail open; never ship an unscreened image.

Scope note: for this 45-minute case, keep the automated gates and the audit log (safety_decisions records the uncertain/manual_review outcome and reviewer/appeal_state) as first-class, but treat the human moderation console and the user-facing appeals workflow as a follow-up — the design routes to review and records the decision, and a separate ops surface acts on that bucket.

view.caption: The API screens prompts through the input safety filter, while GPU workers pass outputs through the output safety filter before storing images in the result store.

steps.decisionPrompt: Why screen both the prompt AND the generated image, not just the prompt?

concepts.definition: Screen inputs before compute and outputs before delivery.
concepts.whyItMatters: Prompts and outputs fail independently; both gates are needed for policy compliance.
concepts.example: Reject a disallowed prompt; also block an unsafe image from an innocuous prompt.
concepts.group: Safety Gates on Input and Output

whyNow: Safety is a hard requirement unique to generative systems; placing it before compute (cost) and before delivery (policy) is the key design point.

recap.after: Input + output safety gates protect cost and policy.
recap.newRisk: Demand far exceeds GPU capacity — jobs must be scheduled fairly across tiers.

traps.why: Models can produce disallowed content from innocuous prompts.

traps.trap: Failing open when the output safety classifier is down or uncertain.
traps.why: An unscreened or ambiguous image can be delivered, violating policy exactly when the safety system is degraded.
traps.instead: Fail closed: block or route to manual review, record the uncertain decision, and keep the job out of the user's hands until resolved.

failureDrills.scenario: The output safety service is down or too slow.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The job holds in a non-terminal state (or routes to review) rather than delivering an unscreened image; the GPU result is retained pending the safety verdict.
failureDrills.mitigation: Fail closed by policy; retry/queue the safety check; alert on classifier availability and uncertain-rate spikes.

steps.title: 4. Priority and Fair Scheduling
description: GPUs are the scarce bottleneck and demand is bursty and multi-tier. Use per-tier priority queues (realtime > paid > free) so high-value/latency-sensitive work goes first, with fair-share so a flood of free requests doesn't starve paid users entirely.

A scheduler pulls from the queues honoring priority + fairness and assigns work to GPU workers. Its inputs per job: tier, remaining user quota, model + size (batchability), deadline/SLO, age in queue, and expected runtime. Quotas/rate limits at intake keep any one user from monopolizing the fleet.

Make fairness measurable, because one 'request' isn't one unit of work — n images, larger resolution, and pricier models cost very differently. Account fair-share in GPU-seconds (or normalized model cost), not raw request count, using per-tier weights with fair-share floors and per-user deficit counters / token buckets. Separate the concerns: priority policy, admission control (quota), batch formation, and worker leasing are distinct.

Assign work via leases, not permanent ownership: a job is leased to a worker with a visibility timeout (the assignment lease) and a scheduler epoch/fencing token; the worker heartbeats while running. If the lease expires (crash, hang), the job is reassigned, and a late completion from the original worker is rejected by epoch so the job isn't double-completed.

Tie the policy to the capacity numbers: realtime/paid get a reserved GPU slice sized to their SLOs (realtime <2 s, paid <30 s p95) and are admission-limited to it, while free gets a fair-share floor that drains from the remaining capacity. When incoming work exceeds the available GPU-seconds — e.g. a viral burst pushing ~400 images/sec against a ~21-GPU steady fleet plus burst pool — realtime/paid stay within their reserved slice and only the free-tier queue grows. Then communicate wait/ETA, throttle, or shed free work rather than dropping accepted jobs or letting free starve paid.

view.caption: The GPU scheduler dequeues from the per-tier priority queues by priority and assigns batched work to the GPU inference workers.

options.name: Per-tier priority queues + fair-share (default)
options.description: Separate queues per tier (realtime, paid, free) feed a priority-aware scheduler with fair-share floors, and per-user quotas cap intake.
pros: Realtime > paid > free ordering keeps latency-sensitive, high-value work ahead of bulk traffic.
Fair-share floors stop a flood of free requests from fully starving paid users.
Quotas at intake cap any one user before work ever reaches the scarce GPUs.
cons: Tuning weights and fair-share floors per tier is fiddly and needs ongoing adjustment.
Several queues plus a priority-aware scheduler is more moving parts than one FIFO.
A persistent paid surge can still push free-tier wait times very high.

view.caption: Per-tier priority queues feed the GPU scheduler, which dequeues by priority and assigns batched jobs to the GPU workers.

options.name: Single FIFO queue + per-user rate limit
options.description: One first-come-first-served queue feeds all GPUs, with per-user rate limits at intake as the only fairness control.
pros: Dead simple: one queue, strict first-come ordering, easy to reason about.
Per-user rate limits at intake still stop one user from monopolizing the fleet.
No priority weights or fair-share math to tune.
cons: A burst of free traffic sits ahead of paid users, so paid latency tracks total queue depth.
No way to express that realtime work should jump the line.
Can't trade fairness for value: every job waits its turn regardless of tier.

view.caption: A single FIFO queue feeds the scheduler first-come-first-served, which assigns batched jobs to the GPU workers.

options.name: Weighted fair queuing (DRR/WFQ)
options.description: A deficit-round-robin/WFQ scheduler gives each tier a weighted, guaranteed share of GPU time tracked by per-tier deficit counters.
pros: Each tier gets a guaranteed share of GPU time proportional to its weight, even under sustained load.
No tier is fully starved: free still drains at its allotted rate while paid stays fast.
Smoother latency distribution than strict priority when all tiers are busy at once.
cons: More complex scheduler state (per-tier deficit counters) than simple priority ordering.
A truly latency-critical realtime job may still wait behind its tier's fair share.
Choosing weights that match business value is non-obvious and load-dependent.

view.caption: The per-tier priority queues feed a weighted fair scheduler that allocates GPU work to workers by each tier's deficit-tracked share.

steps.decisionPrompt: Free users send 100x the volume of paid users. How do you keep paid latency low without starving free entirely?

concepts.definition: Order work by tier priority while guaranteeing each tier some share.
concepts.whyItMatters: Protects high-value latency while avoiding total starvation of lower tiers.
concepts.example: Realtime jumps the queue; free gets a capped slice of GPU time.

whyNow: Scheduling scarce GPUs across tiers is the core resource-allocation problem; fairness is the requirement candidates most often miss.

recap.after: Tiered, fair scheduling of jobs to workers.
recap.newRisk: Even well-scheduled, per-job GPU overhead wastes the scarce resource — batch it.

steps.title: 5. GPU Inference with Batching
description: Running one prompt per GPU pass wastes the hardware: model load and per-call overhead dominate. The scheduler batches compatible jobs (same model/size) into one GPU pass, dramatically raising throughput per GPU.
Workers keep the model weights resident (loaded from the model store once) and process batches; trade a little latency (waiting to fill a batch) for large utilization gains — a tunable batch window.

Batch compatibility is the real constraint: a batch must share model + version, image size, precision, and any LoRA/control settings — version, not just family, because a canary build or different precision can't share a forward pass. Mixed traffic batches poorly, so the scheduler groups by this compatibility key and falls back to smaller (even single-job) passes when no compatible peers are waiting. Cap the batch by GPU memory and close it on a fill-window timeout so the first arrival isn't starved waiting for a full batch; on an OOM, split the batch and retry with a smaller one.

Model version is part of the compatibility key, so it must be pinned before scheduling. Pin it at submit by resolving the model catalog entry (including any canary rollout_pct): this gives reproducibility and lets the scheduler form version-coherent batches. Route hot model+version+size combos to dedicated warm worker pools so common traffic always finds a compatible peer. A canary version draws a small rollout share onto its own pool without contaminating the active version's batches; rollback flips the catalog status so no new jobs schedule onto it while in-flight jobs finish.

Output then goes through the safety filter — typically a separate classifier fleet so the safety model doesn't compete for the diffusion GPUs (co-located only if it fits in spare memory), using the safety policy version compatible with the model that produced the image — and on to the result store. Workers report status back to the job store, recording the model_version actually run.

view.caption: The scheduler assigns batched jobs to GPU workers, which load resident weights from the model store, screen outputs through the safety filter, and store images.

options.name: Dynamic batching with a fill window (default)
options.description: The scheduler waits a short fill window to coalesce compatible jobs (same model and size) into one batched GPU pass.
pros: Coalescing compatible jobs (same model/size) into one GPU pass sharply raises images per GPU-hour.
Weights stay resident, so per-call model-load overhead is amortized across the batch.
A tunable batch window trades a little latency for large utilization gains.
cons: Jobs wait up to the fill window before running, adding tail latency for the first arrival.
Only batches jobs with matching model/size/params, so mixed traffic batches poorly.
A too-large batch can exceed GPU memory and must be capped.

view.caption: The scheduler hands a fill-window batch of compatible jobs to GPU workers that load weights once, then screen and store the images.

options.name: One prompt per GPU pass (no batching)
options.description: Each prompt runs as its own GPU pass with no batching; a free GPU immediately takes the next single job.
pros: Lowest possible latency for a single job — it starts the instant a GPU is free.
Trivial scheduling: assign one job to one GPU, no batch compatibility logic.
Predictable per-job runtime with no batch-fill wait.
cons: Per-call and model overhead dominate, so images per GPU-hour stay low and GPUs are wasted.
Needs far more GPUs to hit the same throughput, blowing up cost.
Bursts overwhelm the fleet quickly since each pass serves only one user.

view.caption: The scheduler assigns one prompt per pass to GPU workers, which load weights and write each result to the result store with no batching.

options.name: Continuous (in-flight) batching
options.description: New jobs join an already-running batch at per-step boundaries rather than waiting for a fixed window to fill.
pros: New jobs join an in-flight batch at step boundaries instead of waiting for a fixed window, keeping the GPU saturated.
Higher sustained utilization than fixed windows when arrival rate is uneven.
Shorter effective wait than a fill window since jobs slot into the running batch.
cons: Requires a model/runtime that supports mid-iteration batch changes — not all diffusion stacks do.
Scheduler must track per-step progress of every in-flight job, adding complexity.
Mixing jobs of very different sizes in one running batch is hard to manage.

view.caption: The scheduler keeps adding jobs to an already-running batch on the GPU workers at per-step boundaries, loading weights once and storing results.

steps.decisionPrompt: How do you raise images-per-GPU-hour without buying more GPUs?

concepts.definition: Grouping compatible requests into one GPU forward pass.
concepts.whyItMatters: Amortizes fixed overhead, multiplying throughput on the scarce resource.
concepts.example: Wait up to 50ms to batch 8 same-size prompts into one pass.

whyNow: Batching is the specific GPU-economics lever; naming the latency-vs-utilization batch window is the staff-level insight.

recap.newRisk: Results are large binaries that many clients fetch — and jobs/workers can fail.

bottlenecks.issue: Cold model load stalls a worker.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Keep weights resident; pin workers to a model; warm pools per model.
Cap batch size by GPU memory; on OOM, split and retry with a smaller batch.
Group by compatibility key; fall back to smaller/single passes; route hot model+size combos to dedicated warm pools.

failureDrills.scenario: A canary model version spikes the blocked-output or OOM rate.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Only the canary's rollout share is affected; the active version keeps serving normally. Rollback flips the catalog status to deprecated/disabled so no new jobs schedule onto the canary, while in-flight canary jobs finish (or re-queue onto the active version) — existing job semantics are preserved.
failureDrills.mitigation: Per-version worker pools and rollout_pct isolate the canary; monitor blocked-output and OOM rate per model_version; rollback is a catalog status flip, not a redeploy.

flows.name: Batched GPU inference + output safety
flows.note: The scheduler hands a batch of compatible jobs to a worker with weights resident; the worker runs the batch, screens each output, and stores safe images while updating job status.

steps.title: 6. Result Delivery
description: Generated images (MBs each) go to object storage; serve them via CDN so the read fanout doesn't hit the workers or origin. The job record holds the result keys/URLs.

Notify the client on completion: a push/stream for realtime UIs, or polling for simple clients. For interactive use, you can stream progress as intermediate diffusion previews. Treat push/SSE/WebSocket as a hint, not a guarantee — on reconnect the client replays state from job status (the durable JobDB record), never from an in-memory stream, so a dropped connection can't lose a completion.

For server-to-server webhooks, sign the payload (HMAC), include a stable event id for consumer idempotency, retry on failure with backoff, and dead-letter after N attempts with operator escalation. Make consumers idempotent on the event id.

Results are user data: serve them through short-lived signed CDN URLs scoped to the owner (per-user signed URLs minted by the API, not public guessable links), with an explicit expires_at returned in the API. Retention and deletion are part of the design — shorter windows for free than paid, and honor user-requested deletion by tombstoning the generated_images row and purging the object + CDN cache.

view.caption: Workers store images in the result store, which the CDN distributes for client fetch, while job completion events flow through the notification/stream to the client.

options.name: Push/stream completion + CDN fetch (default)
options.description: On completion the server pushes a notification over SSE/WebSocket to the client, which then fetches the finished image from the CDN.
pros: A push or SSE/WebSocket stream tells the client the instant a job finishes — best for interactive UIs.
Images served from the CDN keep read fanout off the GPU workers and origin.
Can stream intermediate diffusion previews for a live progress feel.
cons: Maintaining open connections/subscriptions per client adds infrastructure.
Push delivery must handle reconnects and clients that drop offline mid-job.
Overkill for simple clients that just want the final URL.

view.caption: On completion the notification/stream pushes to the client over SSE/WebSocket, and the client fetches the finished image from the CDN-fronted result store.

options.name: Client polls job status
options.description: The client repeatedly polls GET /jobs/{id} until status is done, then fetches the image URL from the CDN.
pros: Dead simple: client polls GET /jobs/{id} until status is done, then fetches the URL.
No persistent connections or push infrastructure to operate.
Robust to client disconnects — it just resumes polling later.
cons: Polling adds latency between completion and the client noticing (poll interval).
Many idle clients polling generate wasteful status-check load.
No live progress; the user sees nothing until the next poll lands.

view.caption: The client polls the job store for status and, once done, fetches the image from the CDN-fronted result store.

options.name: Server-to-server webhook callback
options.description: On completion the service fires a single signed HTTP callback to the caller's hosted endpoint, retrying on delivery failure.
pros: Fires a single callback to the caller's endpoint on completion — ideal for API/integration use.
No client connection to keep open and no polling load.
Naturally fits batch/programmatic consumers that aren't a live UI.
cons: Requires the caller to host a reachable, authenticated endpoint.
Must retry failed deliveries and sign payloads against spoofing.
Poor fit for interactive end-user apps that want live progress.

view.caption: A job-completion event drives the notification layer to POST a signed callback to the caller's webhook endpoint, with the image served via the CDN.

steps.decisionPrompt: Thousands of clients fetch their finished images. What keeps that load off the GPU workers?
whyNow: Delivery completes the async loop; CDN offload + completion notification is the standard, expected result path.

concepts.definition: Store the result, notify/poll for completion, serve via CDN.
concepts.whyItMatters: Decouples large-result reads from the expensive compute tier.
concepts.example: Worker stores image -> job done -> push URL -> client fetches from CDN.

recap.newRisk: Long jobs on a flaky GPU fleet — failures must not lose work or strand GPUs.

steps.title: 7. Reliability, Utilization, and Cost
description: This step has two halves: crash-safe execution (don't lose or double-run a job) and fleet utilization/cost (keep the scarce GPUs busy and cheap). Present them in that order.

Crash-safe execution. Make jobs idempotent and retryable (keyed by job id + attempt): each run is a job_attempts row with a worker lease and heartbeat. A worker crash lets the lease expire, so the job re-enters the queue and another worker re-runs it; the partial output is discarded and a late completion from the dead worker is fenced out by epoch so the job isn't double-completed. Dead-letter jobs past a retry threshold for inspection.

Fleet utilization and cost. Keep GPUs busy: autoscale workers from queue depth (and queue age by tier), keep warm model pools to avoid cold starts — note model-load latency can dominate autoscale response, so scale on a leading signal and pre-warm — and consider cheaper spot/preemptible GPUs for free-tier batch work (with re-queue on preemption).

Track a full operational picture, not just GPU utilization and queue wait, and tie each signal to a concrete response: queue age by tier → scale up / preempt or shed free-tier; OOM rate → lower the batch cap; model cache miss (cold-start) rate → pre-warm the pool for that version/size; batch fill ratio low → widen the fill window or consolidate pools; blocked-output spike → safety incident / canary rollback; retry/dead-letter count → inspect the dead-letter queue; cost per image (attributed by tier and model) → rebalance spot vs on-demand. The whole design exists to keep the scarce, expensive GPUs near-fully utilized while honoring fairness and safety.

view.caption: The priority queues feed the scheduler and GPU workers, which update job status in the job store so a crashed job returns to the queue for retry.

steps.decisionPrompt: A GPU worker dies mid-generation. What happens to that job and that GPU?
whyNow: Closing on reliability + utilization + cost ties the design to the headline metric (don't idle/waste GPUs) and the resilience requirement.

recap.after: Crash-safe jobs; autoscaled, warm, cost-optimized GPU fleet.
recap.newRisk: Spot preemption and autoscale lag — bounded by re-queue, warm pools, and queue-depth scaling.

failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The job re-enters the queue and another worker re-runs it idempotently; the GPU is reclaimed.
failureDrills.mitigation: Idempotent jobs + at-least-once queue + health-driven reassignment.

failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Its in-flight job is re-queued (and re-prioritized if needed).
failureDrills.mitigation: Treat spot capacity as preemptible; re-queue on loss.

failureDrills.scenario: A 'dead' worker finishes late after its job was reassigned.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The stale completion is rejected so the job isn't double-completed or its result double-stored.
failureDrills.mitigation: Lease epoch / fencing token checked on completion; idempotent result write keyed by attempt.

finalDesign.title: Final Design — Text-to-Image Generation Platform
finalDesign.description:
The API screens the prompt (input safety), enforces quota, persists a job, and enqueues it by tier — returning a job id fast. Two invariants anchor the design: (1) every accepted job has exactly one durable JobDB record and a recoverable path into scheduling — the job and an outbox row are written in one transaction and a relay (plus a re-enqueue sweeper) bridges the write→publish gap, with JobDB as source of truth and the queue carrying only job_id; and (2) model_version is pinned at submit from a model catalog, so batches are version-coherent, output safety uses the matching policy version, and canary/rollback is a catalog status flip onto isolated worker pools rather than a redeploy. A fair-share priority scheduler pulls from per-tier queues and assigns batched, compatible jobs (same model+version+size) to GPU workers that keep model weights resident. Outputs pass the output-safety filter, land in object storage, and are served via CDN; the client is notified or polls. Jobs are idempotent and retryable so worker/spot failures re-queue without loss; the GPU fleet autoscales from queue depth with warm model pools to maximize utilization of the scarce, expensive hardware.

Worked example — a paid request for 4 images at 1024px arrives while ~20K free-tier images are backlogged. Intake: idempotency-key checked (new), prompt screened by input safety (allowed), job + 4-image fan-out persisted, enqueued on the paid queue, 202 returned in ~50 ms. Scheduling: the fair-share scheduler favors the paid queue (realtime > paid > free) and charges the user's GPU-seconds budget; the free backlog keeps draining on its floor so it isn't starved but also can't block the paid job. Batch formation: the 4 images (same model + 1024px) coalesce with other compatible paid jobs into a ≤16-image batch, closed by a ~50 ms fill window or memory cap. Inference: a worker with resident weights runs the batch in ~10 s under an assignment lease with heartbeats. Output safety: each image is screened (fail-closed if the classifier is down); safe images are written to the result store with metadata. Delivery: the job is marked done, a push notification (backed by status replay) fires, and the client fetches via short-lived signed CDN URLs. Failure path: if the worker crashes mid-batch, the lease expires, the job re-queues, another worker re-runs it idempotently, and a late completion from the dead worker is fenced out.

view.caption: The API screens prompts, persists and enqueues jobs by tier; a fair-share scheduler batches them onto GPU workers (resident weights from the model store); outputs pass the safety filter into the result store, served via CDN with push notification to the client.

functional.how: Async job: enqueue, GPU inference, deliver via CDN.

nonFunctional.how: Job id returned immediately; processed off the request.
Input + output filters before compute and delivery.
Idempotent retryable jobs; re-queue on crash/preemption.

technologyChoices.concern: API edge, quotas, and abuse controls
tradeoff: A managed API edge is the fastest way to get TLS termination, request shaping, WAF rules, identity hooks, and coarse quota enforcement in front of expensive GPU work.
Application-owned idempotency and safety decisions still belong behind the edge because only the generation API can replay the original response and persist policy audit state.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Edge quotas and WAF rules remove most custom reverse-proxy and coarse abuse-control code, but not per-job quota accounting or safety decisions.

technologyChoices.concern: Authoritative job store, idempotency keys, and transactional outbox
tradeoff: A relational job store gives transactions for jobs, idempotency keys, attempts, generated_images, safety_decisions, and the outbox bridge to queues.
A durable KV store is attractive for very high-rate idempotency and quota counters, but it is weaker as the single lifecycle ledger unless the job state machine is simple.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Conditional writes, unique constraints, TTL, and transactional outbox support remove most hand-built duplicate suppression and stranded-job cleanup logic.

technologyChoices.concern: Priority queue, leasing, and dead-letter dispatch
tradeoff: Queues with visibility timeouts, DLQs, and per-tier streams fit job assignment, retry, and backpressure without making the queue the source of truth.
Kafka-style logs add replay and consumer fanout, but a simpler queue is usually enough when JobDB owns lifecycle and the message carries only job_id plus tier.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Native leases, redelivery, and DLQs remove most custom retry timers and stuck-message sweeper code.

tradeoff: Self-hosted serving gives control over dynamic batching, resident weights, precision, GPU placement, and model compatibility keys.
Managed model endpoints reduce serving operations but can limit batching policy, GPU economics, custom weights, and safety-policy integration.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A fully managed image-model API can remove the worker fleet, scheduler, and batching logic entirely, at the cost of custom-model and utilization control.

technologyChoices.concern: GPU scheduling, autoscaling, and warm pools
tradeoff: Kubernetes plus a batch-aware scheduler makes tier priority, fair-share floors, GPU leases, and warm model pools explicit.
Managed batch services simplify fleet operations, but fair-share accounting and fill-window batching may need to live in an application scheduler anyway.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: GPU-aware autoscaling and queue-age signals reduce the need for a hand-maintained capacity controller, but they do not replace admission control for realtime and paid tiers.

technologyChoices.concern: Model registry, weights store, and rollout control
tradeoff: A model registry plus immutable weight artifacts makes model_version pinning, canary rollout, rollback, safety-policy compatibility, and reproducibility operational rather than tribal knowledge.
Treating weights as ordinary container or object-store artifacts is simple, but the API must still resolve rollout percentage and worker-pool routing from a catalog.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A registry with immutable versions and rollout status removes most custom bookkeeping around canary eligibility, rollback, and artifact provenance.

technologyChoices.concern: Object storage, signed URLs, and CDN delivery
tradeoff: Cloud object storage plus CDN gives durable image retention, signed URLs, lifecycle expiry, global read fanout, and cache invalidation with little operations work.
Self-hosted object storage controls data residency and raw storage cost, but durability, replication, origin shielding, and CDN integration become platform responsibilities.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Object-store lifecycle policies and signed CDN URLs remove most custom retention, expiry, and result-access code.

tradeoff: Managed moderation APIs are fast to adopt and continuously updated, but their policy boundaries may not match product rules, latency targets, or appeal requirements.
In-house classifiers and a review queue give exact policy control and a durable audit trail, but require model maintenance and abuse-operations staffing.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed moderation service can remove early classifier development, but the product still needs safety_decisions, reason codes, and review/audit workflow.

technologyChoices.concern: Completion notification, webhooks, and progress streams
tradeoff: Push, SSE, WebSocket, and webhooks improve perceived latency, but they must be treated as hints backed by durable job status replay.
A broker-backed delivery layer with signed payloads, event ids, retry, and DLQ support is better than relying on in-memory client connections for completion truth.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Managed pub/sub and event delivery remove most custom retry timers and connection fanout plumbing for completion notifications.

technologyChoices.concern: Observability, GPU telemetry, and cost attribution
tradeoff: GPU utilization alone is insufficient; queue age by tier, batch fill ratio, model cache misses, OOM split rate, retry/dead-letter count, and blocked-output spikes need first-class dashboards.
Cloud monitoring is quick to wire, but per-image cost attribution by tier, model, size, and GPU pool still requires deliberate labels/tags and business metrics.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A unified metrics/tracing/cost stack removes most manual fleet inspection and lets autoscaling and incident response act on queue and GPU signals.

say: Confirm async generation on scarce, expensive GPUs.
Pin mandatory safety and multi-tier fairness.
Establish utilization as the headline metric.
Async intake: screen, persist, enqueue, return job id.
Deliver via object store + CDN, notify on completion.
Fair-share scheduling and inference batching for utilization.
Idempotent jobs, autoscaling, warm pools, spot capacity.
Tradeoffs: batch window (latency vs utilization), fairness vs priority, spot cost vs preemption.
Mention progress streaming and model versioning as extensions.

expectations: Async job to a GPU worker, deliver via storage.
May miss batching, fairness, and output safety.
Reasons about batch window and GPU utilization quantitatively.
Designs fair-share across tiers and spot/autoscale.
Discusses warm pools, model versioning, and cost precisely.

followUps: How do you tune the batch window to trade latency for utilization?
How would you stream progress / intermediate previews to the client?
How do you roll out a new model version without downtime?
How would you use spot/preemptible GPUs safely for free-tier work?
How do you stop one user from monopolizing the GPU fleet?
